originalTitle: Lloyds Banking Group to hire 300 tech experts to work on AI
summary: Exclusive: While recruits will increase headcount for now, broader adoption of AI could lead to job cuts in future
content: Lloyds Banking Group has launched an AI recruitment drive for 300 tech experts, weeks before its chief executive, Charlie Nunn, announces a strategic plan for the 261-year-old lender. The bank said it intended the recruits to work on its use and development of agentic AI by September, referring to autonomous AI models that can plan and execute tasks with minimal human oversight. While the hiring drive will increase Lloyds’ headcount for now, the group did not rule out its broad adoption of AI leading to job cuts in the future. The initiative comes as many of the world’s biggest banks adopt AI to simplify processes and cut costs. The Spanish owner of Santander UK said it aimed to save more than £400m by 2028 through automation and hoped to generate another £300m in extra income. All of its 185,000 staff worldwide, including about 15,000 in the UK, will be given access to AI tools. Trystan Davies, the group head of data and AI science at Lloyds, said: “AI will reshape how organisations are structured. It will change roles and how we work, and we are investing in training for colleagues through that transition.” In January, Nunn acknowledged that the bank would have to “reduce some jobs in some areas” owing to AI. Last month, Standard Chartered announced 7,000 job cuts, due in part to AI. Its chief executive, Bill Winters, later apologised for describing the move as “replacing, in some cases, lower-value human capital”. News of Lloyds’ hiring drive comes weeks before Nunn is expected to inform staff and investors of a new multi-year strategy for the banking group next month. He is closing out a current five-year strategy, which included a big push towards online banking involving hundreds of branch closures, as well as a renewed focus on pensions and wealth management. Davies said the AI cohort would be deployed to a range of projects, including identifying and preventing scams and fraud. Some would be working on how AI models could be used internally, including to distill and search reams of documents in the HR department. But one of the key focuses will be on making online banking more accessible and personalised, letting customers interrogate their spending habits, and ask plain language questions about their finances, including which investment versus savings products might best suit their circumstances. “It results in a much better customer experience because our systems are kind of geared up in the right way,” Davies said. The recruits – who will be part of a 1,000-strong AI team also made up of retrained Lloyds staff – will be deploying existing large language models such as Anthropic’s Claude and building on top of public LLMs such as Google’s Gemini to the bank’s own specifications. Lloyds’s AI programme has already delivered financial gains, with generative AI – which creates new content based on patterns in vast, existing datasets – providing a £50m boost to its balance sheet last year. The group expects a £100m benefit this year, thanks to its growing use of agentic AI models. However, research suggests that some UK banks are becoming reliant on AI faster than they are preparing for outages of the technology. KPMG’s latest financial services sentiment survey showed that while 93% of UK bank executives believed they could keep operating in a significant outage, only 47% had carried out a single test around AI disruption, while 26% had not conducted any. Rob Smith, the UK head of regulatory and risk advisory at KPMG UK, said: “The industry’s optimism about its ability to continue business as usual if a critical AI system fails at scale could mean one of three things: one, firms have invested considerably in model validation, contingency planning and risk prevention; two, firms’ use of AI tools is relatively simplistic; orthree, they don’t yet have a complete grasp of their exposure. “Firms have invested time and money, but without regular, robust testing, how do you know what you’re doing is working? And, crucially, how do you prove your resilience to the regulator, customers and stakeholders?”
chineseTitle: 劳埃德银行集团将聘请 300 名技术专家从事 AI 研究
chineseSummary: 独家：虽然目前招聘人数会增加，但人工智能的更广泛采用可能会导致未来裁员
chineseContent: 劳埃德银行集团 (Lloyds Banking Group) 启动了一项针对 300 名技术专家的人工智能招聘活动，几周后，其首席执行官查理·纳恩 (Charlie Nunn) 宣布了这家拥有 261 年历史的银行的战略计划。该银行表示，希望新员工在 9 月份之前参与代理人工智能的使用和开发，指的是能够在最少的人类监督下规划和执行任务的自主人工智能模型。虽然招聘活动目前将增加劳埃德银行的员工人数，但该集团并不排除广泛采用人工智能导致未来裁员的可能性。这一举措出台之际，世界上许多最大的银行都采用人工智能来简化流程并削减成本。英国桑坦德银行的西班牙所有者表示，其目标是到 2028 年通过自动化节省超过 4 亿英镑，并希望再创造 3 亿英镑的额外收入。其全球 185,000 名员工（包括英国的约 15,000 名员工）都将能够使用人工智能工具。劳埃德银行数据和人工智能科学小组负责人特里斯坦·戴维斯 (Trystan Davies) 表示：“人工智能将重塑组织的结构。它将改变我们的角色和工作方式，我们正在投资于为同事提供培训，帮助他们渡过这一转变。”一月份，纳恩承认，由于人工智能的影响，银行将不得不“减少某些领域的一些工作岗位”。上个月，渣打银行宣布裁员 7,000 人，部分原因是人工智能。其首席执行官比尔·温特斯(Bill Winters)后来道歉，称此举“在某些情况下取代了低价值的人力资本”。劳埃德银行招聘活动的消息传出后几周，纳恩预计将在下个月向员工和投资者通报该银行集团的新多年战略。他正在结束当前的五年战略，其中包括大力推动网上银行业务，关闭数百家分行，以及重新关注养老金和财富管理。达夫
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于全球新闻信号，适合用于观察外部环境、政策变化、地缘风险和市场情绪。
